did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including MobileNet, EfficientNet, and DenseNet, to perform multiclass skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative analysis of three CNN architectures (MobileNet, EfficientNet, DenseNet) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology (preprocessing, training splits), quantitative results (accuracy metrics like 94.9%), and new findings regarding class-wise performance, despite being published in conference proceedings.

title: Deep Learning–based Comparative Analysis of CNN Models for Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on AI-Driven Smart Systems and Ubiquitous Computing (ICAUC-2026)
brief_summary: This study performs a comparative analysis of three Convolutional Neural Network architectures—MobileNet, EfficientNet, and DenseNet—for multiclass skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. The models were evaluated across varying training-testing splits (80:20 and 90:10) and multiple epochs to assess performance consistency and generalization. MobileNet achieved the highest overall accuracy of 94.9% at the 90:10 split, followed by EfficientNet at 94.8% and DenseNet at 94.2%. However, detailed class-wise analysis revealed significantly lower precision and recall for clinically critical minority classes such as melanoma and actinic keratosis. The findings highlight the limitation of relying solely on overall accuracy and emphasize the need for robust class-wise evaluation in medical decision support systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for multiclass classification. Identified as a precancerous lesion caused by sun radiation.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a cancer occurring out of the epidermis, usually slow-growing with low chances of spreading.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a set of harmless skin lesions that tend to resemble warts or plaques.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a harmless, firm nodule of skin which often forms after small skin trauma.

One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a malignant tumor of melanocytes and the most violent type of skin cancer with high possibility of spreading. Noted as a clinically critical minority class with lower precision/recall in the study.

One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as benign accumulation of melanocytes (moles). Consistently recorded the highest precision and recall due to dominance in the dataset.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as atypical structures of blood vessels which may be benign or malignant such as angiomas and pyogenic granulomas.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion categories (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic image dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels for seven lesion classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores, with confusion matrices provided for class-wise analysis.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dermatoscopic image dataset comprising seven different types of skin lesions, provided by Kaggle. It contains 10,015 images with significant class imbalance and visual overlap between classes.

use.training: Used for training MobileNet, EfficientNet, and DenseNet models under varying splits (80:20 and 90:10) and epochs (10, 20, 30, 40, 50).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance under varying training-to-testing splits (80:20 and 90:10).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices.

90:10_split.notes: MobileNet achieved the highest overall accuracy. DenseNet showed strong generalization. Detailed class-wise analysis revealed significantly lower precision and recall for clinically critical minority classes such as melanoma and actinic keratosis.

80:20_split.notes: Performance metrics (Precision, Recall, F1-score) were also reported for this split.

70:30_split.notes: EfficientNet reached its highest accuracy of 94.8% at a 70:30 split with only 10 epochs (see 90:10 section for peak comparison context, but Table 2 shows 93.9% at 20 epochs for 70:30 split). Note: Text states EfficientNet hit highest accuracy of 94.8% at 70:30 split with 10 epochs, but Table 2 lists 94.8% for EfficientNet at 70:30 split with 10 epochs. Wait, Table 2 row 10 col 70:30 EfficientNet is 94.8. Correct.

60:40_split.notes: Performance metrics (Precision, Recall, F1-score) were not explicitly detailed in tables for this split in the provided context, only accuracy in Table 2.

class_wise_metrics_90_10_split.notes: Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class consistently recorded the highest precision and recall due to its dominance in the dataset. Clinically critical minority classes such as melanoma (MEL) and actinic keratosis (AKIEC) showed comparatively lower recall values. DenseNet demonstrated superior performance for minority classes.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the models used in this study; literature review mentions ImageNet pretraining for Xception in related work [4] and pretrained convolutional networks for DTLNet [6], but the current study's specific pretraining status is not stated.

MobileNet.backbone: Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks with depth-wise separable convolutions and squeeze-and-excitation (SE) enhancers

layers.first_convolution_stage: 32 filters, kernel size 3x3, stride 2, followed by batch normalization and ReLU6
layers.inverted_residual_stack: Series of inverted residual modules with varied expansion ratios, channel depths, and stride values
layers.terminal_convolution_stage: 1x1 convolutional layer with 1280 filters, followed by global average pooling

MobileNet.hyperparameters: Expansion factors, kernel sizes, strides, and SE ratios vary across blocks

EfficientNet.backbone: Compound scaling strategy uniformly scaling network depth, width, and input resolution
EfficientNet.description: Sequence of convolutional neural networks designed for high predictive performance at lower computational cost

DenseNet.backbone: Dense blocks where each layer has inputs from all preceding layers
DenseNet.description: Proposed by Gao Huang et al. (2017); eliminates vanishing gradient problem, promotes feature reuse, computationally efficient with fewer parameters

training_details.loss_function: Not reported (Softmax activation used for multi-class classification)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.purpose: To give probability scores to each of the seven target skin lesion classes

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including rotation, horizontal flipping, and scaling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of data augmentation to enhance data variability and diminish overfitting. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied as part of data augmentation to enhance data variability and diminish overfitting. Specific probabilities were not reported.
Applied as part of data augmentation to enhance data variability and diminish overfitting. Specific ranges or factors were not reported.

conclusions: MobileNet achieved the highest overall accuracy of 94.9% at a 90:10 training-testing split, followed by EfficientNet (94.8%) and DenseNet (94.2%).
Detailed class-wise analysis revealed significantly lower precision and recall for clinically critical minority classes, specifically melanoma and actinic keratosis, despite high overall accuracy.
Relying solely on overall accuracy is insufficient for medical decision-making due to the risk of misclassifying malignant lesions as benign or vice versa.
The study highlights the challenges posed by class imbalance and visual similarity among skin lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset.
Confusion matrix analysis provided insights into misclassification patterns and their clinical implications, emphasizing the need for reliable class-wise performance in diagnostic systems.
Future work should consider ensemble methods and more effective optimization techniques to enhance accuracy and generalization capabilities.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevi has 6,705 samples vs. Dermatofibroma with 115), potentially biasing model performance toward dominant classes.

Overall accuracy metrics (e.g., MobileNet 94.9%) mask poor performance on clinically critical minority classes; Melanoma and Actinic Keratosis showed significantly lower precision and recall (e.g., Melanoma recall of 0.76-0.81 across models), posing a risk for false negatives in malignant cases.

The paper does not report statistical significance testing (p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance differences between MobileNet, EfficientNet, and DenseNet, limiting the robustness of comparative claims.
Key hyperparameters such as the specific optimizer (e.g., Adam, SGD) and loss function are not explicitly reported, hindering reproducibility.
Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, scaling) are mentioned but specific parameters (angles, probabilities, scales) are not provided.

The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying only on internal train-test splits (80:20 and 90:10) from a single source, which limits generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks or verify that patient-level separation was maintained between training and testing sets, which is critical for dermatoscopic datasets where multiple images may come from the same patient.
No explainable AI (XAI) or interpretability analysis was performed for the proposed models, despite the clinical need for transparency in diagnostic support systems.
The study is published in conference proceedings (ICAUC-2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
